title: Our Research Standards

title: First Week with a New Puppy: A Day-by-Day Guide
h2_new: What should I do to prepare before bringing my puppy home?
answer_text: To prepare for your puppy's arrival, create a calm environment by limiting visitors and new experiences. Establish consistent feeding, toileting, and sleeping schedules from day one, and consider booking a vet appointment to ensure a smooth transition for your new pet.

title: First Week with a New Puppy: A Day-by-Day Guide
h2_original: Day One: Arrival and Settling In
h2_new: How should I handle my puppy's first day at home?
answer_text: On your puppy's first day, ensure a calm journey home by having someone sit with them in the back seat and keeping the car well-ventilated. Upon arrival, take your puppy directly to the designated toilet area before introducing them to their new environment.

title: First Week with a New Puppy: A Day-by-Day Guide
h2_new: What routine should I establish on the second day with my puppy?
answer_text: On the second day, establish a feeding schedule of four meals for puppies aged 8–12 weeks, using the same food recommended by the breeder. Begin toilet training by taking your puppy outside to the same spot regularly, especially first thing in the morning and after meals.

title: First Week with a New Puppy: A Day-by-Day Guide
h2_original: Days Three and Four: Building Confidence
h2_new: How can I help my puppy build confidence during days three and four?
answer_text: To build your puppy's confidence, introduce family members gradually and start name recognition by rewarding them when they respond. Handle your puppy gently and begin introducing a light collar for short periods while continuing toilet training to reinforce positive behaviours.

title: First Week with a New Puppy: A Day-by-Day Guide
h2_new: What can I expect during my puppy's first vet visit on day five?
answer_text: During your puppy's first vet visit, expect a general health check covering weight, heart, lungs, and more. The vet will check the microchip, review vaccination schedules, discuss worming and flea treatments, and provide nutrition advice. Bring your puppy's vaccination record for reference.

title: First Week with a New Puppy: A Day-by-Day Guide
h2_original: Days Six and Seven: Expanding the World
h2_new: What activities should I introduce to my puppy during days six and seven?
answer_text: In days six and seven, start socialising your puppy by carrying them to experience new sounds and sights. Engage in gentle play with appropriate toys, introduce different surfaces for exploration, and allow brief alone time in their safe space to help them adjust.

title: First Week with a New Puppy: A Day-by-Day Guide
h2_new: What are some survival tips for the first week with a new puppy?
answer_text: During the first week with your new puppy, sleep when they sleep to cope with night disruptions. Avoid comparing your puppy's progress to others, accept that accidents will happen, and don't hesitate to ask for help from your vet or trainer. Enjoy this chaotic yet fleeting phase!

title: Puppy Teething Guide: Stages, Signs, and Soothing Tips
h2_new: What is puppy teething?
answer_text: Puppy teething is a natural developmental process where puppies experience pressure, soreness, and irritation in their gums as baby teeth fall out and adult teeth emerge. This phase typically drives intense chewing behaviour, which is common among teething puppies.

title: Puppy Teething Guide: Stages, Signs, and Soothing Tips
h2_new: When do puppies start teething?
answer_text: Puppies begin teething at around 12 weeks old, but the process starts earlier. Baby teeth emerge between 3 to 8 weeks, with incisors appearing first, followed by canines and premolars. By 8 weeks, puppies usually have a full set of 28 baby teeth.

title: Puppy Teething Guide: Stages, Signs, and Soothing Tips
h2_new: What are the signs that a puppy is teething?
answer_text: Signs that your puppy is teething include increased chewing, drooling, red or swollen gums, blood spots on toys, finding baby teeth, reduced appetite, irritability, and face rubbing. These symptoms indicate discomfort as the puppy's teeth develop.

title: Puppy Teething Guide: Stages, Signs, and Soothing Tips
h2_new: How can I redirect my puppy's inappropriate chewing?
answer_text: To redirect inappropriate chewing, calmly say 'no' or 'leave it' when you catch your puppy in the act. Immediately offer an appropriate chew toy and praise them for choosing the right item. Additionally, remove temptations and rotate chew toys to keep them interesting.

title: Puppy Sleep Guide: How Much Sleep Puppies Need by Age
h2_new: What are the sleep needs of puppies?
answer_text: Puppies need a significant amount of sleep for their development, typically sleeping 18–20 hours per day at 8–10 weeks old. Sleep is crucial as it aids in growth, strengthens the immune system, processes experiences, and allows muscles to recover from play.

title: Puppy Sleep Guide: How Much Sleep Puppies Need by Age
h2_new: How much sleep do puppies need by age?
answer_text: At 8–10 weeks, puppies require 18–20 hours of sleep daily, divided into short naps of 30 minutes to 2 hours. They usually sleep 6–8 hours at night, with toilet breaks every 2–3 hours, and may cry during the first week.

title: Puppy Sleep Guide: How Much Sleep Puppies Need by Age
h2_new: Why are daytime naps important for puppies?
answer_text: Daytime naps are essential for puppies as overtiredness can lead to hyperactivity and behavioural issues. Recognising signs of tiredness, such as yawning and glazed eyes, and creating a conducive napping environment can help ensure they get the rest they need.

title: Puppy Sleep Guide: How Much Sleep Puppies Need by Age
h2_new: What should a nighttime sleep routine for puppies include?
answer_text: A nighttime sleep routine should involve winding down activities an hour before bed, offering the last meal at least two hours prior, a final toilet break, and placing the puppy in their bed with a consistent cue. A small treat can reinforce this routine.

title: Puppy Sleep Guide: How Much Sleep Puppies Need by Age
h2_new: How can I create the ideal sleep environment for my puppy?
answer_text: To create an ideal sleep environment for your puppy, provide a comfortable bed suitable for their size, place it in a quiet location away from noise, maintain a consistent temperature of 18–22°C, dim the lighting, and consider using familiar scents or white noise.

title: Puppy Sleep Guide: How Much Sleep Puppies Need by Age
h2_new: What are the signs of sleep problems in puppies?
answer_text: Signs of sleep problems in puppies include sleeping significantly more than expected, difficulty settling despite tiredness, loud snoring or laboured breathing, sudden changes in sleep patterns, and frequent nighttime waking beyond 16 weeks, which may require veterinary attention.

title: Puppy-Proofing Your Home: A Room-by-Room Safety Guide
h2_new: What are the general principles for puppy-proofing your home?
answer_text: To puppy-proof your home, get down to their level to identify hazards, secure all cables using tidies or covers, remove or elevate small objects that could be choking hazards, and use baby gates to block access to potentially dangerous areas.

title: Puppy-Proofing Your Home: A Room-by-Room Safety Guide
h2_new: What should I do to puppy-proof my kitchen?
answer_text: In the kitchen, keep toxic foods like chocolate and onions out of reach, store cleaning products in locked cupboards, use puppy-proof bins, install child-proof locks on low cupboards, and ensure appliances are safely stored to prevent accidents.

title: Puppy-Proofing Your Home: A Room-by-Room Safety Guide
h2_new: How can I create a safe puppy zone in my home?
answer_text: To create a safe puppy zone, use baby gates or a puppy pen to define the area, include their bed and water bowl, ensure the floor is easy to clean, remove all hazards, and gradually expand their access as they mature.

title: Puppy-Proofing Your Home: A Room-by-Room Safety Guide
h2_new: What should I include in my emergency preparedness plan for my puppy?
answer_text: Your emergency preparedness plan should include saving your vet’s emergency number, knowing the location of the nearest out-of-hours vet, saving the Animal Poison Line number, keeping a basic pet first-aid kit accessible, and recognising signs of poisoning like vomiting and seizures.

title: Puppy Vaccination Schedule UK: What to Expect and When
h2_new: What are the core vaccines for UK puppies?
answer_text: Core vaccines for UK puppies include protection against Canine Distemper Virus (CDV) and Canine Adenovirus, which causes infectious hepatitis. These vaccines are crucial as CDV can lead to severe respiratory and neurological issues, while adenovirus targets the liver and kidneys.

title: Puppy Vaccination Schedule UK: What to Expect and When
h2_new: When should puppies receive their first vaccination in the UK?
answer_text: Puppies in the UK typically receive their first vaccination between 6 to 8 weeks old. This initial vaccination often includes the combined DHP vaccine for distemper, hepatitis, and parvovirus, along with the first dose of leptospirosis.

title: Puppy Vaccination Schedule UK: What to Expect and When
h2_new: What is the schedule for booster vaccinations for puppies?
answer_text: In the UK, puppies require a primary vaccination course at 8 and 10-12 weeks of age. The British Veterinary Association recommends a booster vaccination at 12 months, followed by annual or triennial boosters based on the type of vaccine.

title: Puppy Vaccination Schedule UK: What to Expect and When
h2_new: Which non-core vaccines might puppies need?
answer_text: Non-core vaccines for puppies in the UK may include those for kennel cough and other diseases not covered by core vaccines. While core vaccines are essential, non-core vaccines are recommended based on the dog's lifestyle and risk factors.

title: Puppy Vaccination Schedule UK: What to Expect and When
h2_new: What are the common side effects of puppy vaccinations?
answer_text: Common side effects of puppy vaccinations include mild lethargy, reduced appetite, and a small lump at the injection site, which usually resolves within 1-2 weeks. Mild sneezing may occur after kennel cough vaccination, but serious reactions warrant contacting your vet.

title: Puppy Vaccination Schedule UK: What to Expect and When
h2_new: What are the vaccination costs for puppies in the UK?
answer_text: Vaccination costs for puppies in the UK can vary, but the primary vaccination course typically occurs at 8 and 10-12 weeks of age. Additional costs may arise for booster vaccinations at 12 months and subsequent annual or triennial boosters.

title: Dog Health Basics: Common Conditions, Prevention, and When to See a Vet
h2_new: What are the main research sources for dog health information?
answer_text: The main research sources for dog health information include the British Veterinary Association (BVA), the RSPCA, the PDSA, and the Kennel Club. It's important to consult these organisations directly for the most up-to-date guidelines and recommendations.
